ontology.foodex2_description: Biryani — rice-based mixed dish with meat
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Donne Biryani; biryani as a dish class is not individually coded in SNOMED CT as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Chicken Donne Biryani as served at South Indian restaurants in Singapore weighs approximately 550 g and provides a complete meal of spiced rice and chicken.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Chicken Biryani composite; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for chicken (cooked) and short-grain white rice (cooked)
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Donne Biryani is a Karnataka-style short-grain rice biryani. No exact entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA for Donne Biryani specifically. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken biryani composite data and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked short-grain white rice (FDC ID 168878) and roasted/cooked chicken (FDC ID 171477). The input record's per-100g values (energy 154.55 kcal, protein 8.18 g, fat 6.36 g, carbohydrate 16.36 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN chicken biryani reference ranges and are retained with medium confidence. Detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, mineral micronutrients, and moisture data are not individually reported for this composite dish in either ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy; those fields are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (short-grain, cooked) as analogous food; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Donne Biryani. GI estimated at 56 based on short-grain white rice (cooked) GI range of 54–64 (mean ~56) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The presence of chicken protein, yogurt, and whole spices in biryani preparation moderately attenuates the glycaemic response relative to plain rice; GI of 56 (medium) is a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (56 × 16.36 g/100g × 550 g / 100) / 100 = (56 × 89.98 g) / 100 ≈ 50.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 218 mg/100g; a 550 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, which is 52% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to half a plate (approximately 275 g) to keep sodium intake from this meal below 600 mg; pairing with a potassium-rich side such as raita or a vegetable salad can help offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Short-grain Donne rice has a medium-to-high GI (~56) and a 550 g serving delivers approximately 90 g available carbohydrate, producing a glycaemic load of ~50, which is clinically high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request a higher chicken-to-rice ratio, pair with plain yogurt (raita) to slow gastric emptying, and monitor postprandial blood glucose; substituting basmati rice (GI ~50) for Donne rice where available would further reduce glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.18 g/100g and cholesterol 32.73 mg/100g; a 550 g serving provides approximately 12 g saturated fat (60% of the 20 g daily limit) and 180 mg dietary cholesterol.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request minimal cooking oil preparation, choose skinless chicken pieces, and limit portion size to 300 g to keep saturated fat intake from this meal below 6.5 g; the anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, cloves) provides modest cardioprotective benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric/curcumin, ginger, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom) used in material quantities; low saturated fat at 2.18 g/100g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken Donne Biryani is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile; however, those with nightshade sensitivity should monitor for symptom flares as tomatoes and green chillies are present in the preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Donne Biryani is a South Indian-style biryani originating from the Karnataka region of India, particularly associated with Bangalore, and widely served at Indian restaurants in Singapore. The dish is distinguished by its use of Donne rice — a short-grain, slightly sticky variety of rice that absorbs spices more intensely than long-grain basmati — cooked using the dum method, in which marinated chicken and partially cooked rice are layered and slow-cooked together in a sealed vessel until the flavours meld and the chicken becomes tender. The marinade typically includes yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, turmeric, cumin, coriander, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom, and green chillies, producing a deeply aromatic, mildly spiced, and richly flavoured dish. The texture is moist and cohesive, with the rice grains slightly clumped from the short-grain starch, contrasting with the tender, juicy chicken pieces. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 155 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 6.4 g fat (2.2 g saturated), and 16.4 g carbohydrate with 1.1 g dietary fibre. Sodium is moderate at 218 mg/100g. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets. Clinically, the high glycaemic load from short-grain rice makes portion control essential for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the sodium content warrants attention for those with hypertension at full-plate servings; and the anti-inflammatory spice profile (curcumin, ginger, cinnamon) provides modest benefit for inflammatory conditions. The dish is traditionally served in a donne (a cup made from dried leaves) in its Karnataka form, though Singapore restaurants commonly serve it on a plate or banana leaf. It is a popular Friday special at many South Indian eateries in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chicken Donne Biryani?
Is Donne Biryani suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between Donne Biryani and regular biryani?
How much protein in Chicken Donne Biryani?
What rice is used in Donne Biryani?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chicken Donne Biryani exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken biryani composite data and USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked short-grain white rice and cooked chicken, and are consistent with the input record's values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for short-grain white rice. Confidence is therefore medium.

ADDITIONAL NOTES: trans_fat_g is reported at 0.09 g/100g from the input record; this is a trace level consistent with small amounts of cooking oil used in preparation and is retained. The dish name includes '(Only Fridays)' in the source GrabFood listing, indicating a limited-availability menu item; the canonical food name has been normalized to 'Chicken Donne Biryani' without the availability qualifier. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on short-grain white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Donne Biryani was found in peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. Glycemic load per serving (550 g): GL = (56 × 16.36 × 5.5) / 100 ≈ 50.4 — clinically high; portion control is strongly advised for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The ICMR-NIN source URL in the input record points to dietary guidelines rather than the food composition tables directly; the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) is the primary reference used for nutrient adaptation.
